name: Descript
overview: Descript is an AI-powered audio and video editor founded in 2017 by Andrew Mason (co-founder and former CEO of Groupon), spun out of his podcast company Detour. Its defining idea is editing media by editing a text transcript — delete a word from the doc and it's removed from the audio/video. It pioneered Overdub (an AI voice clone for fixing recordings by typing) and includes screen recording, multitrack editing, filler-word removal, and AI features for podcasts and video. Backed by investors including Andreessen Horowitz and OpenAI's Startup Fund, it serves podcasters, YouTubers, marketers, and teams.
strategy: Descript sells tiered SaaS subscriptions (free, creator, pro, enterprise) gated on transcription hours, AI features, and export quality. It is product-led and creator-focused, with strong adoption among podcasters and video teams who value the text-based editing workflow. It monetizes the time savings of AI editing — removing the steep learning curve of traditional editors like Premiere or Audition. Team/collaboration features extend it into business and agency use.
differentiation: Descript's signature edge is text-based editing of audio and video, which collapses a notoriously hard skill into word-processor simplicity — its single most distinctive capability. Overdub voice cloning and AI tools (eye contact, studio sound, filler removal, automatic clips) compound the time savings. It integrates transcription, recording, editing, and publishing in one app rather than a toolchain. The workflow is uniquely approachable for non-editors, which is its durable wedge.
roadmap: Descript is layering more generative and 'agentic' AI into editing — automatic show creation, AI-assisted clip generation, and underlying its own/partner models to make editing increasingly prompt-driven. Expect deeper video capabilities, AI that drafts edits or whole episodes, and continued investment in collaboration. The direction is from a smart editor toward an AI editing co-pilot that does more of the work automatically. Broadening from podcasts into general video creation is a key growth path.
rationale: Mason bet that the bottleneck in audio/video creation was the editing interface itself, and that representing media as editable text would democratize a skill locked behind complex timelines. Building AI features (Overdub, studio sound) on top of that text foundation reflected a belief that the transcript is the right abstraction for AI to operate on. Descript chose the creator/podcast market because those users feel editing pain acutely and adopt time-saving tools eagerly. The thesis: whoever makes editing as easy as writing captures the exploding population of audio/video creators.
references:
Founder: Andrew Mason (CEO)
Flagship: Descript editor, text-based editing, Overdub, Studio Sound
https://www.descript.com
https://www.descript.com/blog

name: Adobe Firefly
overview: Adobe Firefly is Adobe's family of generative AI models and the brand for its generative features, first introduced in March 2023. It spans image generation, generative fill/expand, vector and design generation, text effects, and increasingly video and audio, surfaced both in a standalone Firefly web app and embedded across Photoshop, Illustrator, Express, and Premiere Pro. Adobe's key positioning is that Firefly's image models were trained on licensed Adobe Stock, openly licensed, and public-domain content, making outputs 'commercially safe.' Firefly generations are paired with Content Credentials provenance metadata.
strategy: Adobe monetizes Firefly through a 'generative credits' system tied to Creative Cloud plans and standalone Firefly subscriptions, plus enterprise offerings with IP indemnification. The strategy is to embed generative AI deep inside Adobe's installed base of creative professionals so generation augments existing workflows rather than replacing the tools. For enterprises, Adobe offers custom-trained models and indemnifies customers against IP claims for Firefly-generated content — a commercial-safety guarantee competitors largely can't match. Firefly is also opening to partner models (e.g., third-party image/video models) inside its app while keeping its own commercially-safe core.
differentiation: Firefly's genuine edge is commercial safety and provenance: training on licensed/Adobe Stock/public-domain data, enterprise IP indemnification, and Content Credentials provenance baked in, which matters enormously to brands and agencies wary of copyright risk. Its second edge is deep, native integration into the tools designers already live in (Photoshop generative fill being the standout), making AI a brush rather than a separate app. Adobe also compensates Stock contributors whose work trains the models, building a defensible, ethically-positioned data supply. This 'safe + embedded' combination is the antithesis of scrape-everything rivals.
roadmap: Adobe is expanding Firefly into video, audio, and a broader creative model lineup, and turning the Firefly app into a hub that also hosts select partner models alongside Adobe's own. Expect more agentic and assistive features inside Creative Cloud, deeper enterprise customization, and continued emphasis on provenance and content authenticity standards. Firefly Video and richer design/vector generation are central bets. The direction is an AI layer spanning Adobe's entire creative and marketing stack (including GenStudio for enterprises).
rationale: Adobe bet that for professional and enterprise creatives, legal safety and trust matter more than raw model novelty, so it deliberately constrained training data to licensed/owned sources and added indemnification and provenance. It chose embedding over a standalone-app-only approach because its moat is the millions of professionals already in Photoshop and Illustrator — generation as a feature defends that base against AI-native disruptors. Compensating contributors and championing Content Credentials reflects a thesis that the durable winner in creative AI is the one brands can deploy without copyright fear. Adobe traded the cutting edge of unrestricted models for the larger prize of safe, integrated, monetizable AI across its ecosystem.
references:
Parent: Adobe; CEO Shantanu Narayen
Flagship: Firefly app, Generative Fill (Photoshop), Firefly Image/Video models, Content Credentials

name: Canva
overview: Canva is the Australian design platform founded in 2013 by Melanie Perkins, Cliff Obrecht, and Cameron Adams, and its generative-AI strategy is delivered through 'Magic Studio,' a suite of AI features layered into its drag-and-drop editor. Magic Studio includes Magic Design (auto-generate designs from a prompt or content), Magic Write (AI copywriting), Magic Media (text-to-image and text-to-video), Magic Edit/Grab/Expand, and translation and brand tools. Canva acquired Leonardo.ai in 2024 to bolster its own generative image models and AI talent, and integrates partner models as well. With hundreds of millions of users, Canva embeds AI for the mass market of non-designers.
strategy: Canva monetizes AI by gating Magic Studio's most powerful features behind Canva Pro and Teams/Enterprise subscriptions, using AI as a key driver to convert free users and expand into businesses. Its go-to-market is mass-market and product-led — AI lowers the skill barrier so anyone can produce professional-looking content, reinforcing Canva's core value proposition. For enterprises, it bundles AI with brand controls, collaboration, and its Work Suite to compete with Microsoft and Adobe. The acquisition of Leonardo.ai gives it owned model capability rather than pure reliance on third parties.
differentiation: Canva's edge is distribution and accessibility: it puts generative AI in front of a massive, non-designer audience inside an editor they already use, so AI becomes a one-click shortcut rather than a separate tool to learn. It combines multiple AI capabilities (design, copy, image, video, translate, edit) in a single approachable workflow tied to templates and brand kits. Owning Leonardo.ai plus integrating partner models lets it offer breadth without betting on one model. Its scale and freemium funnel turn AI into a conversion and retention engine that pure-play generators lack.
roadmap: Canva is deepening AI across its Visual Suite — more agentic 'describe it and Canva builds it' design generation, richer video, and enterprise AI with governance. Expect continued buildout of owned models (via Leonardo.ai talent), tighter brand/enterprise controls, and AI woven into docs, websites, and presentations. It is positioning Magic Studio as the AI layer for everyday visual and document work for teams and businesses. The bet is to become the default AI-powered creation suite for the non-specialist.
rationale: Canva bet that the largest opportunity in creative AI is empowering the billions of people who are not professional designers, so it embeds AI to remove skill barriers rather than chasing professional power-users. It chose to integrate AI into its existing high-engagement editor — leveraging distribution as the moat — because reach and ease-of-use convert better than standalone model quality alone. Acquiring Leonardo.ai while also using partner models reflects a pragmatic 'own enough, integrate the rest' stance to avoid dependence on any single provider. The thesis: in mass-market creation, the winner is whoever makes AI effortless inside the tool people already open every day.

name: Figure AI
overview: Figure AI is a Sunnyvale-based humanoid robotics company founded in 2022 by serial entrepreneur Brett Adcock (previously founder of Archer Aviation and Vettery). It builds general-purpose bipedal humanoid robots, progressing from Figure 01 and Figure 02 to the home-oriented Figure 03 announced in October 2025. The robots are driven by Helix, Figure's proprietary vision-language-action (VLA) model that learns tasks from human demonstration video rather than hand-coded scripts. In September 2025 the company closed a Series C of over $1B at a roughly $39B post-money valuation, a steep step-up from its $2.6B Series B.
strategy: Figure pursues full vertical integration of robot hardware and AI, designing its own actuators, batteries, hands, and the Helix model in-house. It launched BotQ, a dedicated manufacturing facility with its own Manufacturing Execution System aimed at scaling to 12,000 units per year and eventually 100,000 over four years. Go-to-market began with commercial and logistics deployments (notably a high-profile BMW Spartanburg pilot) before pivoting toward the home with the lower-cost Figure 03. The business model is selling and leasing robots-as-labor, monetizing physical work hours rather than software seats.
differentiation: Its defining bet is end-to-end vertical integration: in February 2025 Figure ended its collaboration with OpenAI to build Helix internally, with Adcock arguing that solving embodied AI at scale requires owning the full robot-AI stack. Helix runs a fast/slow dual-system architecture (a high-frequency control policy plus a slower reasoning model) entirely on-board, enabling multi-robot collaboration and dexterous bimanual manipulation. Figure 03 introduced soft textile coverings, wireless charging, and tactile sensors fine enough to register a paperclip's weight, signaling a genuine push into safe home operation. The BotQ in-house production line is a manufacturing moat few humanoid rivals have built.
roadmap: Figure is ramping Figure 03 production through BotQ, having moved from roughly one robot per day to one per hour within months, with plans to scale annual output dramatically. It is targeting the home market with a robot priced around $20,000 while continuing commercial logistics deployments. Reports point toward an eventual IPO and a fleet of millions of robots learning collectively from shared data. Continued investment from NVIDIA, Microsoft-adjacent backers, and strategic partners (Brookfield, Qualcomm, T-Mobile, LG, Intel Capital) funds the manufacturing and data-collection flywheel.
rationale: Figure's thesis is that the world faces a structural labor shortage that only general-purpose humanoids can fill, and that the humanoid form factor is the right bet because the physical world is already built for human bodies. Adcock chose vertical integration over relying on a third-party AI lab because he concluded embodied intelligence cannot be solved as a layered API problem; the hardware, sensing, and learned policy must co-evolve. The shift to learning from human demonstration video (Helix) reflects a belief that scalable robot intelligence comes from data and learned policies, not from scripting every behavior. Owning manufacturing is the deliberate counter to the view that robots can be outsourced like phones.
references:
People: Brett Adcock (founder & CEO)
Products/systems: Figure 01, Figure 02, Figure 03, Helix VLA model, BotQ manufacturing facility

name: Physical Intelligence
overview: Physical Intelligence (often styled π) is a San Francisco robotics-AI startup founded in 2024 to build general-purpose foundation models that can control many different robots. Its founding team includes prominent robot-learning researchers Sergey Levine, Chelsea Finn, Karol Hausman, Brian Ichter, and others drawn from UC Berkeley, Stanford, and Google. Its flagship release, π0 (pi-zero), is a vision-language-action flow model for general robot control, followed by π0.5, which extends to open-world generalization for mobile manipulators. By November 2025 the company had raised a $600M Series B at roughly a $5.6B valuation, bringing total funding to about $1.1B.
strategy: Physical Intelligence positions itself as the 'software brain' layer for robotics rather than a hardware maker, aiming to be hardware-agnostic across arms, humanoids, and mobile manipulators. It open-sourced π0's code and weights in early 2025, using openness to seed adoption and gather a broad ecosystem of robot platforms running its policies. Monetization is oriented toward licensing or providing the foundation model to robot builders and enterprises, betting that a single generalist policy beats bespoke per-task engineering. Backers include CapitalG, Lux Capital, Bond, Sequoia, Redpoint, Thrive, OpenAI-adjacent investors, and NVIDIA's NVentures.
differentiation: Its edge is a research pedigree concentrated in the people who pioneered modern robot learning, combined with a deliberate cross-embodiment approach: π0 demonstrated a single model folding laundry, bussing tables, assembling boxes, and making coffee across distinct robot bodies. The flow-matching action architecture lets the model output smooth, high-frequency continuous control rather than discretized tokens, which is well-suited to dexterous manipulation. π0.5's open-world generalization to homes it has never seen is a step toward the 'ChatGPT moment' for robots that pure hardware companies cannot deliver alone. Open-sourcing the model is itself a differentiator in a field where most peers keep weights closed.
roadmap: The company is scaling data collection and training toward broader generalization, with successive model generations (π0, π0.5, and beyond) targeting more autonomy in unstructured environments. It is partnering with robot hardware makers so its policies run on diverse platforms, expanding the data flywheel. The strategic bet is that one foundation model, trained on heterogeneous robot and web data, becomes the default control layer the way LLMs became the default text layer. Expect continued research releases and tighter integrations with humanoid and mobile-manipulator vendors.
rationale: Physical Intelligence's thesis is that intelligence, not hardware, is the bottleneck in robotics, and that the path to general-purpose robots mirrors the foundation-model recipe that worked for language: train one large model on massive, diverse data and let it generalize. The founders chose a cross-embodiment, hardware-agnostic strategy precisely to avoid being locked to a single body form, betting that the model layer captures the most enduring value. They favor learning from large-scale real and web data over hand-engineered controllers because they believe scaling laws apply to physical action too. Open-sourcing π0 reflects a conviction that a shared substrate accelerates the whole field and their own data advantage.
references:
People: Sergey Levine, Chelsea Finn, Karol Hausman, Brian Ichter, Lachy Groom (co-founders)
Products/systems: π0 (pi-zero), π0.5, vision-language-action flow models

name: Skild AI
overview: Skild AI is a Pittsburgh-based robotics-foundation-model startup founded in 2023 by Carnegie Mellon professors Deepak Pathak and Abhinav Gupta. It is building the 'Skild Brain,' which it describes as the industry's first unified, 'omni-bodied' robotics foundation model able to control many different robot bodies without prior knowledge of their exact morphology. In January 2026 Skild raised roughly $1.4B in a round that pushed its valuation past $14B, with SoftBank leading and NVIDIA's NVentures, Bezos Expeditions, Samsung, LG, and others participating. The company reported scaling from zero to about $30M in revenue within months of beginning commercial deployments.
strategy: Skild sells a software brain that can drop into quadrupeds, humanoids, tabletop arms, and mobile manipulators alike, positioning itself as embodiment-agnostic infrastructure rather than a robot manufacturer. Go-to-market spans security and facility inspection, last-mile and point-to-point delivery, warehouses, manufacturing, data centers, and construction. It monetizes by deploying its model onto partners' and customers' robots across these verticals, growing usage-driven revenue. A roster of strategic investors (Samsung, LG, Schneider, Salesforce Ventures, CommonSpirit) doubles as a distribution and pilot pipeline.
differentiation: Skild's signature claim is true omni-body generalization: a single brain that controls a robot it has never seen the body of, contrasting with policies tuned per platform. The founders bring deep academic credibility in self-supervised and reinforcement learning, and the company emphasizes training on extremely large, diverse datasets so the model develops robust, transferable physical skills and emergent recovery behaviors. By refusing to build hardware, it aims to ride the entire robot industry's hardware buildout rather than compete with it. The rapid revenue ramp across many verticals is unusual for a foundation-model robotics startup and signals real-world traction.
roadmap: Skild is using its January 2026 mega-round to scale data collection, training compute, and field deployments across more verticals and robot types. Strategic partnerships with global manufacturers (Samsung, LG, Schneider) point toward embedding Skild Brain in mass-produced robots and industrial settings. The bet is that as humanoids and mobile robots proliferate, an omni-bodied brain becomes the standard control layer they all license. Expect deeper integrations, expanded commercial pilots, and continued model scaling toward broader autonomy.
rationale: Skild's thesis is that the hardware will become commoditized and abundant, so the durable value sits in a single generalist intelligence that can animate any body, the way an operating system runs on any machine. Pathak and Gupta chose the omni-bodied path over per-robot policies because they believe transfer across embodiments, learned from massive scale, is both more capital-efficient and more robust than narrow specialization. Staying out of hardware is deliberate: it lets Skild be the neutral brain the whole ecosystem adopts rather than a competitor to its own customers. The emphasis on large, diverse data reflects a scaling-law conviction applied to physical intelligence.
references:
People: Deepak Pathak (co-founder & CEO), Abhinav Gupta (co-founder)
Products/systems: Skild Brain (omni-bodied robotics foundation model)

name: Tesla AI
overview: Tesla AI is the artificial-intelligence arm of Tesla, spanning the Optimus humanoid robot, Full Self-Driving (FSD), and Tesla's custom AI silicon. Optimus is a general-purpose bipedal humanoid first shown at AI Day in 2022 and iterated through Gen 2 and Gen 3 prototypes; FSD is the vision-only driver-assistance and autonomy stack shipping in millions of Tesla vehicles; and the chip program runs from in-car inference processors (HW3/HW4/AI4) toward the next-generation AI5. Dojo was Tesla's in-house training supercomputer built around custom D1 silicon. All three are driven by a shared end-to-end neural-network philosophy and a fleet-scale real-world data advantage.
strategy: Tesla's AI strategy is to harvest data from a global fleet of millions of camera-equipped vehicles, train large neural networks on it, and deploy improved policies back over the air, monetizing FSD through a software subscription and one-time purchase. Optimus is positioned to reuse the same vision, planning, and actuation know-how and AI compute, with the long-term aim of selling humanoid labor at scale, first inside Tesla's own factories. The company designs its own inference chips to control cost and performance, and Musk has framed both robotaxis and Optimus as potentially the largest sources of future Tesla value. Vertical integration of vehicle, robot, chip, and AI model is the throughline.
differentiation: Tesla's genuine edge is fleet scale: no competitor has hundreds of thousands to millions of cars continuously gathering edge-case driving data, which feeds FSD's end-to-end, camera-only approach that deliberately forgoes lidar and HD maps. The same data pipeline, simulation, and AI compute infrastructure can be repurposed for Optimus, giving Tesla a manufacturing and supply-chain base (motors, batteries, actuators, cells) that pure-play robot startups lack. Custom silicon lets Tesla co-design model and hardware. FSD v14 advanced to a larger end-to-end model on AI4 hardware, though it remained supervised rather than fully unsupervised.
roadmap: Tesla taped out its AI5 chip in April 2026 (well behind the original schedule); Musk stated AI5 deploys to Optimus and supercomputer clusters first rather than to cars, with AI6 to follow via a Samsung partnership. The Optimus V3 reveal slipped to mid-to-late 2026 with first external commercial customers targeted around year-end, featuring more joints, a refined drive system, and improved balance. Tesla wound down the original Dojo program in mid-2025 to consolidate on a unified AI5/AI6 platform, though Dojo 3 discussions were later revived. FSD continues iterating toward unsupervised autonomy and the broader robotaxi rollout.
rationale: Tesla's thesis is that real-world autonomy and humanoid labor are fundamentally data and compute problems best solved by a vertically integrated company that already builds the bodies, the sensors, and the silicon, then collects unmatched real-world data at fleet scale. It chose camera-only, end-to-end neural networks over lidar-plus-HD-maps because Musk argues human driving proves vision suffices and that the mapping approach does not scale globally. Optimus exists because Tesla believes the hardest part of a useful humanoid is the AI and actuation it already develops for cars, making the robot a natural extension. The pivot away from Dojo toward AI5/AI6 reflects a judgment that a unified inference-and-training silicon platform is more capital-efficient than a separate bespoke supercomputer.
references:
People: Elon Musk (CEO), Tesla AI / Autopilot engineering teams
Products/systems: Optimus (Gen 2 / V3), Full Self-Driving (FSD v14), Dojo / D1 chip, AI4 and AI5 inference silicon

name: Wayve
overview: Wayve is a London-based autonomous-driving company founded in 2017 by Alex Kendall and Amar Shah, spun out of University of Cambridge machine-learning research. It champions 'AV2.0,' an embodied-AI, end-to-end learned approach to driving that aims to work on any vehicle, anywhere, without HD maps or hand-coded rules. Its core research includes the GAIA family of generative world models and the LINGO language-action models. In 2024 it raised a $1B+ Series C led by SoftBank, and in early 2026 it added a roughly $1.2B Series D, reaching about an $8.6B valuation with backers including Microsoft, NVIDIA, Uber, Mercedes-Benz, Nissan, and Stellantis.
strategy: Wayve sells its 'AI Driver' software to automakers and mobility operators rather than running its own robotaxi fleet, positioning itself as an embedded autonomy supplier for mass-produced vehicles. It is hardware-agnostic and map-free, designed to generalize across vehicle platforms, sensor suites, and geographies, lowering the cost of expanding to new cities and countries. Monetization comes through OEM licensing deals (notably integrating into Nissan's next-generation ProPILOT) and robotaxi platform partnerships such as the Uber/NVIDIA DRIVE Hyperion effort. Its commercial path runs from supervised consumer-vehicle autonomy to robotaxi trials.
differentiation: Wayve's distinguishing bet is fully end-to-end learned driving without the HD maps, lidar dependence, or rule-based stacks that define classic AV approaches, making it cheaper to scale to new locations. Its GAIA generative world models can synthesize realistic, controllable driving video from text, action, and video prompts, used both to train policies and, with GAIA-3, to evaluate and stress-test autonomy on rare edge cases. The LINGO models add language interfaces and explainability to the driving policy. This 'foundation-model for driving' framing, backed by Microsoft and NVIDIA, separates it from geofenced, map-heavy rivals.
roadmap: Wayve plans to launch commercial robotaxi trials in 2026 and to ship supervised autonomy software in consumer vehicles starting in 2027. With Nissan, it is integrating the Wayve AI Driver into next-generation ProPILOT, with the first equipped model targeted for Japan around fiscal year 2027, and is previewing an Uber robotaxi pilot in Tokyo on NVIDIA DRIVE Hyperion. It continues scaling GAIA world models as a foundation for safety evaluation and training. The strategic thrust is global, OEM-embedded autonomy across many vehicle types.
rationale: Wayve's thesis is that driving is an embodied-AI problem that should be learned end-to-end from data, not engineered through brittle, map-dependent, rule-based pipelines that fail to generalize beyond their geofence. Kendall's team chose the AV2.0 approach because they believe a single learned model that improves with data and works anywhere is the only path to global scale and acceptable cost. Generative world models like GAIA are central to that bet, since safely validating a learned driver requires generating the long tail of scenarios reality rarely supplies. Licensing to OEMs rather than owning fleets reflects a belief that the durable value is the AI driver, deployable across the world's existing cars.
references:
People: Alex Kendall (co-founder & CEO), Amar Shah (co-founder)
Products/systems: Wayve AI Driver, AV2.0, GAIA / GAIA-3 world models, LINGO

name: World Labs
overview: World Labs is a spatial-intelligence company co-founded in 2024 by Stanford AI pioneer Fei-Fei Li (creator of ImageNet) alongside Justin Johnson, Ben Mildenhall, and Christoph Lassner. It builds 'large world models' that generate and reason about persistent, navigable 3D environments rather than text or 2D images. Its first commercial product, Marble, takes text prompts, photos, video clips, panoramas, or rough 3D layouts and produces editable, explorable 3D worlds. World Labs emerged from stealth in September 2024 with about $230M at a $1B valuation and in February 2026 raised roughly $1B more from AMD, Autodesk, Emerson Collective, Fidelity, NVIDIA, and Sea, totaling around $1.23B.
strategy: World Labs is commercializing spatial intelligence through Marble, targeting creators, game and 3D designers, simulation, robotics training, and the broader 'metaverse'/3D content pipeline. It monetizes by selling access to world generation as a product and platform, positioning 3D world models as the next foundational modality after language and image. Strategic backers like Autodesk and AMD signal go-to-market into professional 3D, design, and simulation tooling. The company frames itself as the infrastructure layer for AI that must understand and act in physical space.
differentiation: Its genuine edge is the team's foundational credibility in computer vision and 3D (ImageNet, neural rendering / NeRF lineage via Mildenhall) applied to a modality most AI labs ignore: persistent, geometrically and physically coherent 3D worlds. Marble's outputs are not just images but consistent, editable, navigable environments, addressing the consistency and persistence problems that 2D video models struggle with. Li's framing of spatial intelligence, that true world models must understand and interact with worlds semantically, physically, geometrically, and dynamically beyond what LLMs reach, defines a distinct research program. Few competitors combine this depth in 3D with this caliber of founding team and capital.
roadmap: World Labs is scaling Marble from limited beta into broader commercial release and expanding the kinds of inputs and richer, more dynamic worlds it can generate. The fresh roughly $1B round funds larger models and deeper integrations with design, simulation, gaming, and robotics-training customers. Strategically it bets that spatial/world models become essential infrastructure for both content creation and for training and grounding embodied AI agents. Expect continued model generations and tooling that move from static scenes toward more interactive, physically grounded environments.
rationale: World Labs' thesis is that intelligence is fundamentally spatial, that language alone cannot capture how the world is laid out, behaves, and can be acted upon, so the next frontier is models that natively generate and reason in 3D. Li chose world models over scaling text or 2D-video systems because she argues spatial intelligence is a prerequisite for robotics, AR/VR, simulation, and any AI that must operate in the physical world. The decision to ship a concrete product (Marble) rather than pure research reflects a belief that 3D world generation is already commercially valuable to creators and engineers today. It is a deliberate bet that the 3D modality, not just bigger LLMs, is where durable value accrues.
references:
People: Fei-Fei Li (co-founder & CEO), Justin Johnson, Ben Mildenhall, Christoph Lassner (co-founders)

name: Sakana AI
overview: Sakana AI is a Tokyo-based AI research lab founded in 2023 by David Ha, Llion Jones, and Ren Ito. Jones is a co-author of the landmark 'Attention Is All You Need' transformer paper, and Ha is a former Google Brain and Stability AI researcher; the name 'Sakana' means fish, evoking schools/swarms as a metaphor for collective, nature-inspired intelligence. Rather than training ever-larger frontier models from scratch, Sakana pioneers evolutionary and collective methods, including 'evolutionary model merge' that breeds new capable models by combining existing ones. In November 2025 it raised a $135M Series B at roughly a $2.635B valuation, making it one of Japan's most valuable startups, with total funding past $479M.
strategy: Sakana positions itself as a capital- and energy-efficient alternative to the brute-force scaling race, building smaller, smarter, composable models and AI agents tailored for Japan and enterprise use. It monetizes through enterprise partnerships and custom AI development, with notable deals in finance (MUFG, Daiwa Securities) and plans to expand into industrial, manufacturing, and government sectors. Its national-champion positioning, Japanese-language and Japan-market focus, and backing from investors including NEA, Khosla, Lux, and the CIA-linked In-Q-Tel give it a distinctive strategic footing. The model-merging approach lowers compute costs, a deliberate commercial differentiator.
differentiation: Sakana's real edge is a contrarian research thesis: evolutionary, swarm, and merging techniques that produce strong models without the enormous GPU clusters frontier labs depend on. Its work includes the 'AI Scientist,' an agentic system that autonomously generates research ideas, runs experiments, and writes papers, plus evolutionary model merge and collective-intelligence methods inspired by natural systems. This nature-inspired, efficiency-first stance directly attacks the energy and cost ballooning across the industry. The founding pedigree (a transformer co-inventor) lends unusual credibility to a small-models, big-ideas strategy.
roadmap: Sakana plans to expand its enterprise business beyond finance into industrial, manufacturing, and government sectors through 2026, building on its Japanese-corporate partnerships. It continues advancing evolutionary methods, autonomous-research agents, and collective-intelligence systems as its core research bets. Strategically it aims to be Japan's foundational AI lab and a global proof that efficiency-driven, nature-inspired approaches can rival scale-driven ones. Expect more agentic and self-improving systems alongside deeper enterprise deployments.
rationale: Sakana's thesis is that nature solves intelligence through evolution, diversity, and collective behavior rather than monolithic scale, so AI should too, breeding and combining many smaller models instead of training one giant one. Ha and Jones chose this path partly out of conviction and partly out of pragmatism: a Tokyo startup cannot out-spend OpenAI or Google on compute, so it competes on cleverness and efficiency. They bet that as energy and GPU costs constrain the scaling race, evolutionary and merging techniques become not just cheaper but genuinely more adaptive. The Japan focus reflects a belief that a local, sovereign, culturally fluent AI champion has durable demand the global giants underserve.
references:
People: David Ha (co-founder & CEO), Llion Jones (co-founder & CTO), Ren Ito (co-founder & COO)

name: Isomorphic Labs
overview: Isomorphic Labs is an AI drug-discovery company spun out of Google DeepMind in 2021 and led by DeepMind co-founder and CEO Demis Hassabis. Its founding premise is that the structure of biology can be modeled computationally, building on the AlphaFold breakthrough; it co-developed AlphaFold 3 with DeepMind to predict protein structures and the interactions of proteins with DNA, RNA, and small molecules. In March 2025 it raised its first external funding of $600M, led by Thrive Capital with GV and Alphabet participating. Its stated ambition is nothing less than to 'solve all disease' by designing drugs with AI.
strategy: Isomorphic operates a dual model: partnered drug-discovery programs with major pharmaceutical companies (Eli Lilly and Novartis) that bring upfront and milestone payments, alongside internal proprietary programs focused primarily on oncology and immunology. It monetizes through these pharma partnerships and aims to capture more value over time by advancing its own AI-designed candidates into the clinic. The strategy is to build a next-generation AI drug-design engine that compresses the time and cost of discovery, then license or co-develop the resulting molecules. Alphabet's backing gives it deep capital and compute alongside DeepMind research ties.
differentiation: Its genuine edge is privileged access to and co-development of frontier structural-biology AI (AlphaFold 3) plus DeepMind's research culture and talent, an advantage no conventional pharma or biotech startup can replicate. By modeling protein-ligand and broader biomolecular interactions in silico, it aims to design and optimize candidate molecules computationally before committing to expensive wet-lab and clinical work. The 'isomorphism' thesis, that the same deep mathematical structures recur across biology and can be learned, frames a fundamentally different, AI-first approach to discovery. Nobel-laureate leadership (Hassabis and John Jumper won the 2024 Chemistry Nobel for AlphaFold) anchors its scientific credibility.
roadmap: Isomorphic is advancing its first wholly AI-designed candidates toward human clinical trials; at Davos in January 2026 Hassabis pushed the first-trial timeline from end of 2025 to end of 2026, reflecting the realities of clinical readiness. It is deploying its $600M raise to scale the drug-design engine and progress oncology and immunology programs. The strategic bet is to prove that AI-originated molecules can succeed in the clinic, then expand across disease areas and deepen pharma partnerships. Long term it aims to industrialize AI drug design into a repeatable engine spanning many therapeutic targets.
rationale: Isomorphic's thesis is that drug discovery has been bottlenecked by slow, costly trial-and-error chemistry, and that AI which truly understands biological structure can rationalize and accelerate the whole pipeline, ultimately making disease a tractable computational problem. Hassabis chose to spin a focused company out of DeepMind because applying AlphaFold's structural insight to therapeutics requires dedicated drug-development expertise, pharma partnerships, and clinical infrastructure DeepMind itself was not built for. It bets on an AI-first design engine over traditional high-throughput screening because predicting interactions computationally promises orders-of-magnitude gains in speed and breadth. The partner-plus-internal model hedges near-term revenue against the larger prize of owning AI-designed medicines.
references:
People: Demis Hassabis (founder & CEO), John Jumper (AlphaFold, DeepMind collaborator)
Products/systems: AlphaFold 3, AI drug-design engine; partnerships with Eli Lilly and Novartis

name: Waymo
overview: Waymo is the autonomous-driving company under Alphabet, originating as the Google Self-Driving Car Project started in 2009 and rebranded Waymo in 2016. It operates Waymo One, a fully driverless commercial robotaxi service, and develops the Waymo Driver, a sensor-rich autonomy stack combining lidar, radar, and cameras with HD maps. By early 2026 it was providing on the order of 400,000 paid rides per week across multiple U.S. metros and had surpassed tens of millions of lifetime rides. In February 2026 it raised about $16B at a roughly $126B valuation, with Alphabet remaining the majority backer.
strategy: Waymo runs and scales its own robotaxi fleet rather than licensing software, monetizing through per-ride fares in expanding service areas and via partnerships (e.g., Uber for fleet access in some markets). It pursues a methodical, safety-first, city-by-city expansion, validating each new geography before opening to the public, and partners with Magna to manufacture purpose-built vehicles. The model is capital-intensive but builds a defensible operational moat: real driverless miles, regulatory relationships, depot operations, and a recognized consumer brand. It is now extending to freeways and airports to broaden the service's usefulness.
differentiation: Waymo's real edge is being the clear leader in actually deployed, fully driverless, paid public rides at scale, far ahead of rivals on cumulative autonomous miles and operational maturity. Its redundant multi-sensor stack (lidar plus radar plus cameras) and detailed mapping are engineered for high safety margins and have produced a strong real-world safety record that underpins regulatory trust. Years of operating experience across depots, remote assistance, fleet management, and city permitting form a hard-to-copy operational system. Alphabet's capital, compute, and AI research provide durable backing few competitors can match.
roadmap: Waymo opened to riders in additional U.S. cities (including Dallas, Houston, San Antonio, Orlando, Austin, and Miami) and is laying groundwork for ride-hailing in over 20 more cities, including its first international markets of Tokyo and London, in 2026. It introduced freeway rides to public riders in Phoenix, San Francisco, and Los Angeles in late 2025 and added airport service. With Magna it is expanding its Arizona plant to build thousands more vehicles. The stated target is around 1 million rides per week by the end of 2026, roughly quadrupling current volume.
rationale: Waymo's thesis is that safety and trust are won by deploying a heavily redundant, well-mapped, sensor-rich system and proving it over millions of real driverless miles before scaling, rather than rushing a cheaper, vision-only stack to market. It chose to own and operate fleets because it believes the value and the safety case come from end-to-end control of vehicles, operations, and the rider experience, not from selling software to others. The deliberate, city-by-city expansion reflects a conviction that each new domain must be earned through validation and regulatory partnership. Backed by Alphabet's patience and capital, Waymo bets that operational excellence and a safety lead compound into a durable robotaxi network.

name: Boston Dynamics
overview: Boston Dynamics is a pioneering robotics company founded in 1992 by Marc Raibert as an MIT spinout, now owned by Hyundai Motor Group (which acquired a controlling stake from SoftBank in 2021). It is renowned for dynamically capable robots: the agile bipedal Atlas, the quadruped Spot, and the warehouse robot Stretch. In 2024 it retired its hydraulic Atlas and unveiled a fully electric, commercially oriented Atlas, and at CES in January 2026 it presented the production version during Hyundai's media day. The company has long set the bar for robot mobility, balance, and dexterity.
strategy: Boston Dynamics commercializes Spot (industrial inspection and data capture) and Stretch (case handling and unloading) as products today, while developing electric Atlas as its bet on general-purpose humanoid labor. Under Hyundai, its go-to-market increasingly leverages the parent group's manufacturing scale, supply chain (Hyundai Mobis actuators), and factory deployment opportunities. It monetizes through robot sales, fleet software, and support, and is pivoting Atlas from a research showpiece toward paying industrial use. Early Atlas deployments are committed to Hyundai's Robotics Metaplant Application Center and to Google DeepMind.
differentiation: Boston Dynamics' enduring edge is unmatched mastery of whole-body dynamics, balance, and high-performance actuation, decades of mobility engineering that newer humanoid entrants are still catching up to. It is now fusing that hardware excellence with modern learned intelligence: a Toyota Research Institute partnership on Large Behavior Models gave Atlas autonomous whole-body manipulation, and a Google DeepMind collaboration integrates foundation models for higher-level cognition. A partnership with the Robotics & AI Institute (led by founder Marc Raibert) adds a shared reinforcement-learning pipeline. Backing from Hyundai supplies industrial muscle, actuator supply, and real factory deployment sites few rivals can match.
roadmap: Boston Dynamics began production of the electric Atlas at its headquarters, with all 2026 Atlas units already committed, shipping to Hyundai's RMAC and to Google DeepMind. It is deepening AI partnerships (Toyota Research Institute LBMs, Google DeepMind foundation models, RAI Institute reinforcement learning) to give Atlas broad, learned manipulation and reasoning. Strategically it is moving Atlas from demonstration to industrial deployment inside Hyundai's manufacturing footprint, while continuing to grow Spot and Stretch commercially. Hyundai's CES 2026 robotics strategy frames Atlas as central to its broader robotics and mobility ambitions.
rationale: Boston Dynamics' thesis is that truly useful humanoids require world-class physical capability first, robots that can move, balance, and manipulate reliably, and that learned intelligence should be layered onto that hardware foundation rather than excused by it. Historically it prioritized dynamics and control because it believed athletic competence was the hard, defensible part; now it partners for AI (Toyota, Google DeepMind, RAI) because it recognizes that scalable autonomy needs modern learned behavior models, not hand-tuned control alone. Going electric and commercial reflects a judgment that the market for industrial humanoid labor has arrived. Hyundai ownership was chosen to pair that engineering with the manufacturing scale and capital needed to actually ship robots.
references:
People: Robert Playter (CEO), Marc Raibert (founder, now leading the Robotics & AI Institute)
Products/systems: Atlas (electric humanoid), Spot (quadruped), Stretch; partnerships with Toyota Research Institute (LBM), Google DeepMind, and Hyundai
